<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247063324 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00311: (2026x) Authentication by certificate

- page_id: `247063324`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247063324/2026x+Authentication+by+certificate
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-14T11:25:11.728+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) Configuration > (2026x) Authentication server > (2026x) Common Access Card
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following parameters are utilized to authenticate the user by using certificates, e.g., certificates from CAC authentication.

| Parameter | Required | Description | Default value |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| authentication.certificate.enabled | no | An option that enables or disables certificate authentication. Available values are true and false. | false |
| authentication.certificate.headers.enabled | no | An option that enables or disables certificate authentication when authentication server is under proxy. Available values are true and false. This option by default is hidden and should be used only if authentication server is not accessible without a proxy.Appropriate HTTP headers should be sent by the proxy:X-Issuer-DnX-Subject-DnX-Serial-Number (hex format) | false |
| authentication.certificate.username.source | no | Source of the username. Available values: dn (if the username is constructed from the Subject Distinguished Name) or san (if the username is constructed from the Subject Alternative Name). | dn |
| authentication.certificate.username.san.type | no | If authentication.certificate.username.source value is san, this parameter specifies the type of SAN to use. Available values:0 - other name,1 - RFC 822 name,2 - DNS name,4 - directory name (in case of this type parameter authentication.certificate.username.template is required). | 4 |
| authentication.certificate.username.san.id | no | SAN identifier to use if SAN type is 0. For example, if SAN -> Other Name -> Principal Name is to be used, the ID should be set to "1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.3". | - |
| authentication.certificate.username.template | no | Template that is used to create username from the subject DN (Distinguished Name) or SAN (Subject Alternative Name) of type 4 stored on the certificate. Required if certificate authentication is enabled.The template can contain ASCII characters as well as placeholders in round brackets that are replaced with relative distinguished name (RDN) values from the DN. | (CN) |
| authentication.certificate.displayname.source | no | Source of the display name on login page. Available values: dn (if the username is constructed from the Subject Distinguished Name) or san (if the username is constructed from the Subject Alternative Name). | dn |
| authentication.certificate.displayname.san.type | no | If authentication.certificate.displayname.source value is san, this parameter specifies the type of SAN to use. Available values:0 - other name,1 - RFC 822 name,2 - DNS name,4 - directory name (in case of this type parameter authentication.certificate.displayname.template is required). | 4 |
| authentication.certificate.displayname.san.id | no | SAN identifier to use if SAN type is 0. For example, if SAN -> Other Name -> Principal Name is to be used, the ID should be set to "1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.3". | - |
| authentication.certificate.displayname.template | no | Template that is used to create a display of the subject DN (Distinguished Name) or SAN (Subject Alternative Name) of type 4 stored on the certificate. Required if certificate authentication is enabled.The template specified the same way as username template, except that display name is used for display purposes only. Display name is shown on authentication button that enables the user to authenticate with the selected certificate. | (CN) |
| authentication.certificate.revocation.list.file | no | The absolute path to the certificate revocation list (CRL) file, if it is stored on the filesystem. Multiple files are available if there are several certificate revocation lists.If there are multiple files, separate each file by a comma (for example, <Path and file name 1>,<Path and file name 2>, <Path and file name 3>). | - |
| authentication.certificate.revocation.list.url | no | The URL of the certificate revocation list (CRL) file, if it is available on the web. Multiple URLs are available if there are several certificate revocation lists.If there are multiple URLs, separate each URL by a comma (for example, <URL1>,<URL2>, <URL3>). | - |

#### PANEL: A configuration example

A configuration example

```text
authentication.certificate.enabled=trueauthentication.certificate.username.source=sanauthentication.certificate.username.san.type=4authentication.certificate.username.template=(O)-(CN)authentication.certificate.displayname.source=dnauthentication.certificate.displayname.template=(CN)'s Smart Card
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The following parameters are utilized to authenticate the user by using certificates, e.g., <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c5ba8e5a-6caf-4721-87b7-546a8de2426a">certificates from CAC authentication.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 100.0%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.8013%;" /><col style="width: 5.3787%;" /><col style="width: 68.7706%;" /><col style="width: 5.0494%;" /></colgroup><thead><tr><th><p>Parameter</p></th><th><p>Required</p></th><th><p>Description</p></th><th><p>Default value</p></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>authentication.certificate.enabled</td><td>no</td><td><p>An option that enables or disables certificate authentication. Available values are <strong>true</strong> and <strong>false</strong>.</p></td><td>false</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.headers.enabled</td><td>no</td><td><p>An option that enables or disables certificate authentication when authentication server is under proxy. Available values are <strong>true</strong> and <strong>false</strong>. This option by default is hidden and should be used only if authentication server is not accessible without a <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="944c181f-7525-4cfc-8603-20e433bc8745">proxy</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</p><p>Appropriate HTTP headers should be sent by the proxy:</p><pre>X-Issuer-Dn<br />X-Subject-Dn<br />X-Serial-Number (hex format)</pre></td><td>false</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.username.source</td><td>no</td><td>Source of the username. Available values: <strong>dn</strong> (if the username is constructed from the Subject Distinguished Name) or <strong>san</strong> (if the username is constructed from the Subject Alternative Name).</td><td>dn</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.username.san.type</td><td>no</td><td><p>If <em>authentication.certificate.username.source</em> value is <strong>san</strong>, this parameter specifies the type of SAN to use. Available values:</p><p><strong>0</strong> - other name,</p><p><strong>1</strong> - RFC 822 name,</p><p><strong>2</strong> - DNS name,</p><p><strong>4</strong> - directory name (in case of this type parameter <em>authentication.certificate.username.template</em> is required).</p></td><td>4</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.username.san.id</td><td>no</td><td><p>SAN identifier to use if SAN type is <strong>0</strong>. For example, if <em>SAN -&gt; Other Name -&gt; Principal Name</em> is to be used, the ID should be set to &quot;1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.3&quot;<em>.</em></p></td><td>-</td></tr><tr><td><p>authentication.certificate.username.template</p></td><td>no</td><td><p>Template that is used to create username from the subject DN (Distinguished Name) or SAN (Subject Alternative Name) of type 4 stored on the certificate. Required if certificate authentication is enabled.</p><p>The template can contain ASCII characters as well as placeholders in round brackets that are replaced with relative distinguished name (RDN) values from the DN.</p></td><td>(CN)</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.displayname.source</td><td>no</td><td>Source of the display name on login page. Available values: <strong>dn</strong> (if the username is constructed from the Subject Distinguished Name) or <strong>san</strong> (if the username is constructed from the Subject Alternative Name).</td><td>dn</td></tr><tr><td>authentication.certificate.displayname.san.type</td><td>no</td><td><p>If <em>authentication.certificate.displayname.source</em> value is <strong>san</strong>, this parameter specifies the type of SAN to use. Available values:</p><p><strong>0</strong> - other name,</p><p><strong>1</strong> - RFC 822 name,</p><p><strong>2</strong> - DNS name,</p><p><strong>4</strong> - directory name (in case of this type parameter <em>authentication.certificate.displayname.template</em> is required).</p></td><td>4</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">authentication.certificate.displayname.san.id</span></td><td>no</td><td><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">SAN identifier to use if SAN type is <strong>0</strong>. For example, if <em>SAN -&gt; Other Name -&gt; Principal Name </em>is to be used, the ID should be set to &quot;1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.3&quot;.</span></p></td><td>-</td></tr><tr><td><p>authentication.certificate.displayname.template</p></td><td>no</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Template that is used to create a display of the subject DN (Distinguished Name) or SAN (Subject Alternative Name) of type 4 stored on the certificate. Required if certificate authentication is enabled.</p><p>The template specified the same way as username template, except that display name is used for display purposes only. Display name is shown on authentication button that enables the user to authenticate with the selected certificate.</p></div></td><td>(CN)</td></tr><tr><td><p>authentication.certificate.revocation.list.file</p></td><td>no</td><td><p>The absolute path to the certificate revocation list (CRL) file, if it is stored on the filesystem. Multiple files are available if there are several certificate revocation lists.</p><p>If there are multiple files, separate each file by a comma (for example, &lt;Path and file name 1&gt;,&lt;Path and file name 2&gt;, &lt;Path and file name 3&gt;).</p></td><td>-</td></tr><tr><td><p>authentication.certificate.revocation.list.url</p></td><td>no</td><td><p>The URL of the certificate revocation list (CRL) file, if it is available on the web. Multiple URLs are available if there are several certificate revocation lists.</p><p>If there are multiple URLs, separate each URL by a comma (for example, &lt;URL1&gt;,&lt;URL2&gt;, &lt;URL3&gt;).</p></td><td>-</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f32a2e3d-f2ca-4ddb-a335-cb267ba9c33b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">A configuration example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>authentication.certificate.enabled=true<br />authentication.certificate.username.source=san<br />authentication.certificate.username.san.type=4<br />authentication.certificate.username.template=(O)-(CN)<br />authentication.certificate.displayname.source=dn<br />authentication.certificate.displayname.template=(CN)'s Smart Card</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247063289 space=MCS version=2 -->
## PAGE 00312: (2026x) Authentication server

- page_id: `247063289`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247063289/2026x+Authentication+server
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-16T10:33:56.667+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) Configuration
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Teamwork Cloud authentication server is built to facilitate authentication service. Applications can validate and authenticate entities (such as remote users or nodes) that attempt to access the network using account names and passwords.

Authentication server can also create external users in the Teamwork Cloud system once they have been validated successfully. You can allow the authentication server and the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing LDAP servers' space=''] to do this using Teamwork Cloud Admin's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Managing server settings' space=''] page.

- This section pro vides the configuration parameters of Authentication server. You can find these parameters in Teamwork C loud *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties* fil e. SAML configuration parameters are managed in the Web Application Platform's **Settings** > **SAML** page.
- Authentication server properties can be encrypted. To encrypt these properties, follow the identical procedure for [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Encrypting property values' space=''].

Authentication server installation has changed since earlier versions. Now, Authentication server is placed under the Web Application Platform and thus no longer runs as a separate service.

The following sections provide instructions on how to install and configure the authentication server in Teamwork Cloud.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target">Teamwork Cloud authentication server is built to facilitate authentication service. Applications can validate and authenticate entities (such as remote users or nodes) that attempt to access the network using account names and passwords.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target">Authentication server can also create external users in the Teamwork Cloud system once they have been validated successfully. You can allow the authentication server and the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing LDAP servers" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[LDAP server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to do this using Teamwork Cloud Admin's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Managing server settings" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Server Settings]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="886bb431-9d59-42c0-a24d-d603321d9b5d"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="fe021dbe-75e6-4ee2-856c-023eddd7dbf3">This section pro<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">vides the configuration parameters of Authentication server. You can find these parameters in </span>Teamwork C<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">loud <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties</em></span> fil<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">e. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">SAML configuration parameters are managed in the Web Application Platform's <strong>Settings</strong> &gt; <strong>SAML</strong> page.</span></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Authentication server properties can be encrypted. To encrypt these properties, follow the identical procedure for <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Encrypting property values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[encrypting property values of Web Application Platform]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4b0c730-c810-4d2c-aee3-ee3531af4539"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Authentication server installation has changed since earlier versions. Now, Authentication server is placed under the Web Application Platform and thus no longer runs as a separate service.<br /></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following sections provide instructions on how to install and configure the authentication server in Teamwork Cloud. </p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="0c1b2873-9c37-4334-9b2f-c0b543b95db7" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303988766 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00313: (2026x) Automated installation using scripts

- page_id: `303988766`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/303988766/2026x+Automated+installation+using+scripts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-27T16:29:13.898+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) Monitoring Stack
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Downloads**

Download the *twc-scripts-<version>-linux-monitoring-stack.zip* script package [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html).

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves setting up a monitoring node and at least one telemetry node. The monitoring node consists of a database for storing metrics, Telegraf for pulling metrics from Teamwork Cloud, and the Cassandra database, with a web interface based on Grafana for real-time data visualization. The installation script installs Telegraf to stream metrics tothe monitoring node. We recommend deploying the monitoring node on a dedicated server. Single-node deployment of the Monitoring Stack and Teamwork Cloud is possible, provided there are enough system resources for all components.

wget is not part of the installation script. Install it using the following command:

```text

```

##### Installing Monitoring Stack

**Single-node deployment (Cassandra, Teamwork Cloud, and Monitoring Stack installed all on the same server)**

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves downloading themonitoring stack installation scripts, running them on all nodes, and configuring visualization.

1. [Download](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts)and extract *monitoring-stack_<version>.zip**.*
2. Add execute permission to all .sh files extracted, if they are not set.
3. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
4. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh* inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
5. Navigate to the *Telemetry-Node-Install* folder.
6. Execute*install_telemetry_node.sh.*Provide either the fully qualified domain name, or the IP address of the server.
7. Stop **twcloud**service.
8. Restart **cassandra**service.
9. Start**twcloud**service.
10. Proceed to import and configure the Grafana dashboard below.

**Multi-node deployment (Cassandra/Teamwork Cloud on one server, and Monitoring Stack installed on a separate server)**

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves downloading the monitoring stack scripts, extracting the scripts, and then using install_monitoring_node.sh to install the monitoring stack software on that node. Then the script install_telemetry_node.sh can be run to install the telemetry software on any node with Teamwork Cloud and/or Cassandra installed.

1. On the monitoring node (node that is to host the Grafana interface), [download](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts)and extract *monitoring-stack_<version>.zip*on the monitoring node (the node with the Grafana interface)*.*
2. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
3. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh*inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
4. Transfer the*Telemetry-Node-Install*folder to each telemetry node.
5. On each telemetry node:
  1. Navigate to the Telemetry-Node-Install folder.
  2. Stop **twcloud**service.
  3. Execute*install_telemetry_node.sh*inside this folder. Provide the IP address or fully qualified domain name for the monitoring server.
  4. Restart **cassandra**service.
  5. Start**twcloud**service.
6. Proceed to import and configure the Grafana dashboard below.

##### Importing Grafana dashboard

To set up Grafana dashboard

1. Go to the Grafana home page at http://<monitoring-node>:3000 (note the HTTP address) in a web browser.
2. Log in to the Grafana dashboard. Use default credentials of admin/admin for new installation; You will be prompted to change the admin password after initial login.
3. From the home page, locate the Dashboards menu on the left menu and select Import: 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click the Upload JSON File button. Locate and select the Grafana dashboard file called *Teamwork Cloud Dashboard.json* from the monitoring stack package.
5. Map the telegraf data source to Telegraf as show below. Click **Import.** [ATTACHMENT filename='import_twc_dashboard.png']
6. To set this dashboard as the default Home Dashboard, go to Configuration and select Preferences. Select the Teamwork Cloud dashboard under Home Dashboard and save.

The monitoring node installation script creates a guest user, with credentials guest/guest, who can view the dashboard but cannot make any changes to it.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247064995 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00314: (2026x) Available report types

- page_id: `247064995`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247064995/2026x+Available+report+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-14T11:26:54.742+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Reports application
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are 7 types of reports available in the Reports application:

- The **Access change**is a list of users’ actions related to resources. Exported data shows which users were granted or lost access to a resource, the time of the action, and who performed it.
- The **Failed login attempts** report is self-explanatory; it shows all failed logins. This is an essential report for IT administrators who audit server security.
- The **Locked resources** report shows a list of resources which has locked elements.
- The **Resource access** report shows a timeline of selected users who have accessed various resources in a given time frame.
- The **Resource modifications** report is a list of users who have modified resources in a given time frame with all essential information, such as the commit message.
- The **Resource revision**report is an Excel spreadsheet with the history of a Teamwork Cloud project. You can share this type of report as a quick and easy option to demonstrate progress to stakeholders.
- The **Security audit**report shows all users with access and their details, such as who and when granted access, the scope of user assignment, and so on.
- The **User details** report allows you to export user data, such as their full name, phone number, or email. Also, you can choose to export user permissions, which is helpful for user audit purposes.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Reports application.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">There are 7 types of reports available in the Reports application:</span></p><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a1040346-08e7-4a69-9465-4c7f7f1e2d70">Access change</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a1040346-08e7-4a69-9465-4c7f7f1e2d70"> i</ac:inline-comment-marker>s a list of users’ actions related to resources. Exported data shows which users were granted or lost access to a resource, the time of the action, and who performed it.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Failed login attempts</strong> report is self-explanatory; it shows all failed logins. This is an essential report for IT administrators who audit server security.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Locked resources</strong> report shows a list of resources which has locked elements.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Resource access</strong> report shows a timeline of selected users who have accessed various resources in a given time frame.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Resource modifications</strong> report is a list of users who have modified resources in a given time frame with all essential information, such as the commit message.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Resource revision </strong>report is an Excel spreadsheet with the </span></span>history of a Teamwork Cloud project. You can share this type of report as a quick and easy option to demonstrate progress to stakeholders.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The <strong>Security audit </strong>report shows all users with access and their details, such as who and when granted access, the scope of user assignment, and so on.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>User details</strong> report allows you to export user data, such as their full name, phone number, or email. Also, you can choose to export user permissions, which is helpful for user audit purposes.<br /><br /></span></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:width="930"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reports_app.png" /></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Reports application.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247064713 space=MCS version=7 -->
## PAGE 00315: (2026x) Backup and restore data procedures

- page_id: `247064713`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247064713/2026x+Backup+and+restore+data+procedures
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2026-01-08T08:52:39.272+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Administration Guide > (2026x) Product administration
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Scripts**

This installation uses the following script files. [Download](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/latest/magic-collaboration-studio/downloadable-scripts-283246728.html) and extract the *backup_restore_scripts_<version>.zip* script package to access the files.

- backup.sh
- backup_all.sh
- restore-single_node.sh

This page provides the instructions for cold and hot backups, and the scripts required to perform hot backup and restore of the Cassandra database. These backup and restore scripts should be executed on a Linux server where Cassandra 4 is running.

##### Cold Backup/Restore

A cold backup is a backup taken via regular system backups, while the database is shut down in a consistent state. Once the database is shut down in a consistent state, we back up the directories pointed to by the **data_file_directories** and **commitlog_directory** values in your **cassandra.yaml** file.

Backup procedure consists of:

- Stop the WebApp service
- Stop the Teamwork Cloud service
- Stop the Authserver service
- Commit all the data to disk by issuing the "nodetool drain" command
- Stop the Cassandra service
- Perform the backup (this can be a VM snapshot, a backup of the specified directories using any backup software, or copying the directories to another location)
- Start the Cassandra service
- Start the Teamwork Cloud service
- Start the Authserver service
- Start the WebApp service

Restore procedure consists of:

- Stop the WebApp service
- Stop the Teamwork Cloud service
- Stop the Authserver service
- Stop the Cassandra service
- Delete the data_file_directories and commitlog_directory
- Restore the data from the backup to the original location
- Start the Cassandra service
- Start the Teamwork Cloud service
- Start the Authserver service
- Start WebApp service

##### Hot Backups

###### Backups

Hot backups are backups which are taken while the database is running, therefore eliminating the need for downtime. This is accomplished by using Cassandra's internal ability to perform a snapshot of the database. The backup process consists of flushing data to disk, stopping any compactions which may be taking place, creating a snapshot (the snapshot creates a set of hard links to the current immutable files), archiving the state of the immutable files to a backup location, and clearing the snapshots directories created in the database. For disaster recovery, this directory should be located on a different physical drive than where the data resides. Each snapshot will consume approximately the same amount of space as the data directory. The backup script does not perform any directory maintenance or purging. This can be done either by using a wrapper script which will delete the current backup before creating the new snapshot, or by a scheduled task which is to be executed before the backup. If point in time recovery is desired, the backup directory can be backed up as part of the regular backup procedures for the server.

When backing up a cluster, all nodes must be backed up individually, preferably at the same time. **Also, on a cluster, it is imperative that the clocks on all machines be synchronized.**

Hot backups should be executed at a time when there is no client activity, in order to ensure model consistency. If you cannot be sure that there is no client traffic at the time of the snapshot, please stop the Teamwork Cloud service momentarily. Once the “nodetool snapshot” command has completed, you can restart the Teamwork Cloud service.

###### Restore

The restore procedure will stop the Cassandra database, delete the commit logs and the data files in the repository, restore the snapshot files and relocate them back to their source directories. Once this is done, Cassandra will be started and the nodetool utility will be invoked to repair the keyspaces.

When restoring a cluster, all nodes must be restored from their individual backups (backups taken at the same time). You must first stop the Teamwork Cloud, authserver, and Cassandra service on each of the nodes. Once the Cassandra nodes are off, proceed to restore the seed node. Once you have completed the restoration of the seed node, proceed to restore the second node. Upon completion of the second node, restore the third node. During the restoration process of the first 2 nodes, you may receive an error from the keyspace repair process - this is normal since some of the nodes have not joined the cluster. The last node to be restored will take care of the keyspace repairing process.

##### Backup and restore scripts

scripts

To backup and restore the Cassandra database, you need the *backup.sh*and *restore-single_node.sh*files from the *backup_restore_scripts_<version>.zip* script package. Download the package [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCS/Downloadable+scripts).

###### backup.sh

This script creates snapshots of Cassandra database. The script must run on the host running Cassandra and Cassandra must be running. The Cassandra bin directory or nodetool must be added to **$PATH** (on Linux, nodetool is available when the Cassandra package is installed without further modifications). The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner. On Linux, you should be running on a shell with root permissions.

The command format for backing up is:

```bash

```

where **CASSANDRA_DB_PATH** is the directory below where the Cassandra data is located, and **BACKUP_DIR** is the location in which the snapshot archives are to be saved.

The archive is named using a pattern **cassandra_backup_yyyy.mm.dd-hh.mm.ss.tar**

If any of the parameters are omitted then the script will prompt you for the locations, and perform sanity checks to make sure the values are correct.

#### NOTE: Path names

Path names

When calling the backup/restore scripts passing parameters, if your path names contain spaces, they must be quoted.

For example:

./backup.sh -dir "/path to data containing spaces/data" -rf "/path to backups containing spaces/"

###### restore-single_node.sh

This script should be run as root on Linux and Cygwin console must be launched with the administrator privileges. This script restores data from the backup file. While restoring Cassandra, Teamwork Cloud server must be turned off. The script stops and starts Cassandra if needed. The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner, and then restores backup on another machine with another user. Therefore, the file permissions must be changed manually otherwise Cassandra may not start. The following variables may be passed onto the script:

```text
] [--commitlog ] [--rf ] [--cassandra ] [-h|--help]
--dir: cassandra database path (default:cassandra_database)
--commitlog: commitlog path (default: commitlog_path)
--rf: full path to snapshot archive (default: rf)
--cassandra: cassandra installation path when cassandra is not executed as a service (default: cassandra)
-h,--help: Prints help]]>
```

Even though parameters can be passed to the restore script, restores are typically carried out in an interactive manner.

| Variable | Description |
| --- | --- |
| commitlog_path | The path of commitlog files. They are removed when restoring database to prevent recovery of commits made earlier. DataStax recommends to store the commitlog files in a separate hard-disk for performance sake. |
| cassandra_database | The Cassandra database path. It is used to clean existing database and restoring database from the recovery file. |
| cassandra | The Cassandra installation path. It is the required parameter when Cassandra is launched not as service. |
| service | The Boolean parameter indicating that Cassandra is started as a service. The default value is true so you need to set this variable to false when Cassandra is launched by the other script or manually. |
| rf | The recovery file with the full path to it. |

###### **On Linux**

Type:

```bash

```

or

```bash

```

If you are logged in as root, the command is:

```bash

```

command</li><li data-uuid="6003f733-55ee-4ed0-b60a-07ec73879058">Stop the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="27dbda46-8fba-4815-b198-0adb980fa59b">Perform the backup (this can be a VM snapshot, a backup of the specified directories using any backup software, or copying the directories to another location)</li><li data-uuid="d0969a93-ffc8-46a8-8a6f-41fadb543ff6">Start the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="2d077dbb-10fa-4538-b911-48b782a95e7a">Start the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="547cf004-cf02-4466-a5e8-655050b010a4">Start the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="b81d69fa-3e6a-4200-94e9-021a4c96a7b0">Start the WebApp service</li></ul><p>Restore procedure consists of:</p><ul><li data-uuid="6a2c999a-bcdf-40ab-a726-94c5168c8aa8">Stop the WebApp service</li><li data-uuid="3cb1339c-0eb2-4be1-aa40-4682394248cc">Stop the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="3d24cb6b-c7f5-4816-a485-c34a0ab6a5a0">Stop the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="5f6e7371-236c-43bf-a88a-1b10524ea95a">Stop the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="726e0155-bc7e-43f0-88d7-bf92ead17f9f">Delete the <strong>data_file_directories</strong> and <strong>commitlog_directory</strong></li><li data-uuid="12f81108-1ce0-4f50-a40e-bbf4ba681c5e">Restore the data from the backup to the original location</li><li data-uuid="50691fdc-407a-4077-ab54-06d3cb5b20c3">Start the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="b9616036-dea9-45b1-b469-6cb68db2bfa9">Start the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="cab720b4-bd07-4940-a003-eb119a2fa4c6">Start the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="f006c279-0ed5-4e4b-8f4b-161ea0af2f66">Start WebApp service</li></ul><h3>Hot Backups</h3><h4>Backups</h4><p>Hot backups are backups which are taken while the database is running, therefore eliminating the need for downtime. If point in time recovery is desired, the backup directory can be backed up as part of the regular backup procedures for the server.</p><p>When backing up a cluster, all nodes must be backed up individually, preferably at the same time. <strong>Also, on a cluster, it is imperative that the clocks on all machines be synchronized.</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b410d9f-9414-467d-b3b2-fd62a0e88135"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Hot backups should be executed at a time when there is no client activity, in order to ensure model consistency. If you cannot be sure that there is no client traffic at the time of the snapshot, please stop the Teamwork Cloud service momentarily. Once the “nodetool snapshot” command has completed, you can restart the Teamwork Cloud service.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Restore</h4><p>The restore procedure will stop the Cassandra database, delete the commit logs and the data files in the repository, restore the snapshot files and relocate them back to their source directories. --commitlog: commitlog path (default: commitlog_path)
````html
--rf: full path to snapshot archive (default: rf)
--cassandra: cassandra installation path when cassandra is not executed as a service (default: cassandra)
-h,--help: Prints help]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Even though parameters can be passed to the restore script, restores are typically carried out in an interactive manner.</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup class=""><col class="" /><col class="" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th>Variable</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>commitlog_path</strong></td><td>The path of commitlog files. They are removed when restoring database to prevent recovery of commits made earlier. DataStax recommends to store the commitlog files in a separate hard-disk for performance sake.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>cassandra_database</strong></td><td>The Cassandra database path. It is used to clean existing database and restoring database from the recovery file.</td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>cassandra</strong></td><td>The Cassandra installation path. It is the required parameter when Cassandra is launched not as service.</td></tr><tr class=""><td colspan="1"><strong>service</strong></td><td colspan="1">The Boolean parameter indicating that Cassandra is started as a service. The default value is true so you need to set this variable to <em>false</em> when Cassandra is launched by the other script or manually.</td></tr><tr class=""><td colspan="1"><strong>rf</strong></td><td colspan="1">The recovery file with the full path to it.</td></tr></tbody></table><h4><strong>On Linux</strong></h4><p>Type: </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9227c5ec-45ce-40da-9d33-37928be9e017"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ su ./restore-single_node.sh ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">or</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06f669d0-dce6-4748-9ac2-7902a51e4326"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ sudo su
$ ./restore-single_node.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you are logged in as root, the command is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9988a4a2-2a97-4cb7-8457-5ae3ef3970ef"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ ./restore-single_node.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247048419 space=MCS version=3 -->
## PAGE 00317: (2026x) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `247048419`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247048419/2026x+Cameo+Collaborator+for+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-14T12:12:54.372+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) User Guide
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud is a web-based product designed to present models in a simplified form for stakeholders, sponsors, customers, and engineering teams. It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.

Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:

800450

Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:

- Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.
- Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.
- Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.
- Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.
- Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.
- Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.

To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud is a web-based product designed to present models in a simplified form for stakeholders, sponsors, customers, and engineering teams. It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:<br /><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="41338db9-c745-4d83-a11f-a0eb646f7bcd"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">800</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YE6GCoJVHeg" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">450</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p class="bodytext" style="text-align: left;">Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><p>Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.</p></li><li><p>Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.</p></li><li><p>Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.</p></li><li><p>Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.</p></li><li><p>Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.</p></li><li><p>Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements</p></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="high_level_architecture.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.</h6><p>To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="1af390c3-7a92-4d14-bb6a-49fc02d6d986" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247064401 space=MCS version=6 -->
## PAGE 00319: (2026x) Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud licensing

- page_id: `247064401`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247064401/2026x+Cameo+Collaborator+for+Teamwork+Cloud+licensing
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-09-26T20:48:44.191+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) License management
- labels: ['twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### License types

You can purchase the following types of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork licenses, depending on the number of connections you want to use:

| License types | Authenticated commenting | Anonymous commenting | Number of concurrent authenticated connections | Access to content shared for anonymous use* | Predefined portals | Customized portals |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 5-connection license | + | - | 5 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 10-connection license | + | - | 10 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 25-connection license | + | - | 25 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 50-connection license | + | - | 50 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 75-connection license | + | - | 75 | Unlimited | + | + |
| 100-connection license | + | - | 100 | Unlimited | + | + |
| Unlimited license | + | + | Unlimited | Unlimited | + | + |

***** Unauthenticated users can open Cameo Collaborator documents via [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Sharing document links' space=''].

#### Applying licenses to new installations (no existing license server)

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses a floating license managed by the FlexNet license server or DSLS (Dassault Systèmes License Server). The license server information is specified when installing Web Application Platform with the Cameo Collaborator service, so make sure that you install a license server before installing Web Application Platform. The steps below explain how to apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license and start using the product.

To apply the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license

1. Install and start one of the following license servers:
  - [DSLS](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=dsls-installation-and-licensing&version=latest)
  - [FlexNet](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-license-server-installation-and-licensing&version=latest)
2. Check if the license server is running.
3. Add the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license to the license server.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine' space=''] with the Cameo Collaborator service and specify the license server details during the installation.

Once you apply the license as described above, the product is ready to use. Note that you can change the license server details later if needed.

#### WARNING: Working with multiple licenses

Working with multiple licenses

The FlexNet license server can contain only one license. If you want to add multiple licenses to the same FlexNet license server (e.g., Cameo Collaborator, Teamwork Cloud, and MagicDraw), contact your sales executive and ask them to merge the licenses.

#### Managing Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud licenses

There are multiple ways to manage Cameo Collaborator licenses

#### NOTE: Multiple pods' licensing

Multiple pods' licensing

If you have multiple pods (i.e. Kubernetes), you can configure all licenses either via the*webappplatform**.properties*file OR via the user interface in the **Settings**application.

If you want to manage licenses via the user interface in the **Settings**application, but at least one pod has the license configured via the *webapp.properties*file, you need to remove the license from the *webapp.properties*file and restart the services.

###### Applying a license through Teamwork Cloud Admin

To apply a Cameo Collaborator license through Teamwork Cloud Admin

1. Open Teamwork Cloud in a web browser using this address format: http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp . 8443 is the default port.
2. Sign in to Teamwork Cloud with a user who has the Server Administrator role .
3. Click the Settings application to open it.
4. From the left-side menu, select the Cameo Collaborator license page. [ATTACHMENT filename='cc_license_in_webapp.png']
5. Select either the FlexNet or DSLS tab:
  - In the FlexNet tab, enter the license server address to view the list of available licenses. [ATTACHMENT filename='cc_flexnet_license.png'] Select a license and click Confirm to apply.
  - In the DSLS tab, select either Organization defined or Custom.
    - If you select Organization defined, choose a license from the list and click Confirm to apply.
    - If you select Custom, enter the license server address, choose a license from the list and click Confirm to apply.

###### Viewing active users

The Cameo Collaborator license page allows you to view active connections (users). They are displayed in the **Recently active connections**part of the page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Managing licenses via the properties file

###### Checking which license is in use

You can check Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud license information either or as described below.

##### To check the license information

1. In your internet browser, go to *http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp*. Default portBy default, Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.
2. If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.
3. In the same internet browser, go to *http(s)://<domain_name>:<port>/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/info*

The web page displays Cameo Collaborator license details, including the number of connections and expiration date.

###### Specifying which license should be used

If there are several licenses in the license server, you can specify which Cameo Collaborator license (edition) should be used.

To specify which Cameo Collaborator license should be used

1. Go to the *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf* directory.
2. Open for editing the *webappplatform.properties* file.
3. In the file, add the **cc.license.edition** property and specify its value (e.g., **cc.license.edition=100+ Connections** for an unlimited license). Valid property valuesThe valid values for the **cc.license.edition** property are:5 Connections10 Connections25 Connections50 Connections75 Connections100 Connections100+ Connections
4. Restart the Web Application Platform.

###### Resetting a license

The instructions below explain how to reset the Cameo Collaborator license if, for example, the connection with the license server was lost.

To reset the Cameo Collaborator license

1. In an internet browser, go to *http(s)://<server_address>:<port>/webapp*. Default portBy default, Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud uses port 8443.
2. If you are not signed in, enter your user credentials on the authentication page.
3. In the same internet browser, go to *http(s)://<domain_name>:<port>/collaborator/api/collaborator/license/reset*

**If the license reset was successful, the open web page will display this message:

{"initialized":true}

###### Specifying license server information for Web Application Platform

The licensing information should be specified when installing Teamwork Cloud and/or Web Application Platform. If you have not specified licensing information during installation or if you want to change it later, you can do this by following the steps below.

To specify/change the license server information

1. Go to the *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf* directory.
2. Open for editing the *webappplatform.properties* file. In the *webappplatform.properties* file:The values of some properties are placeholders, e.g., **mail.from.default=info@company.com**. Make sure to replace the placeholders with actual values.Read the comments with property descriptions to ensure accuracy when specifying property values.
3. Specify the following properties under the FlexNet/DSLS server properties for Web Application Platform services property group.
  - For the DSLS server:
    - **license.framework**
    - **license.server**
    - **license.dsls.server.mode**
  - For the FlexNet server:
    - **license.framework**
    - **license.server** Cameo Collaborator supports the [FlexNet Three-Server Redundancy mode (Triad License Server)](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-three-server-redundancy---triad-license-server&version=latest). If you want to use the Triad License Server, specify the **license.server** property in this format:license.server=<port>@<server1_address>,<port>@<server2_address>,<port>@<server3_address>
4. Restart Web Application Platform.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

message:</p><p>{&quot;initialized&quot;:true}</p><h4>Specifying license server information for Web Application Platform</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The licensing information should be specified when installing Teamwork Cloud and/or Web Application Platform. If you have not specified licensing information during installation or if you want to change it later, you can do this by following the steps below.</span></p><p><br /></p><p>To specify/change the l<span>icense server information</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="62060814-d152-4951-96e6-35ba2ad5e794"><p>Go to the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf</em> directory.</p></li><li data-uuid="34e2081c-9046-4f31-a1c4-d28a0c750f48"><p>Open for editing the <em>webappplatform.properties</em> file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e24de815-3242-4ef4-8043-eedd63e07e43"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <em>webappplatform.properties</em> file:</span></p><ul><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The values of some properties are placeholders, e.g., <strong>mail.from.default=info@company.com</strong>. Make sure to replace the placeholders with actual values.</span></p></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Read the comments with property descriptions to ensure accuracy when specifying property values.</span></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="894ee1c5-a99a-4625-934d-cfc1d960a142">Specify the following properties under the <em>FlexNet/DSLS server properties for Web Application Platform services</em> property group.<ul><li><p>For the DSLS server:</p><ul><li><p><strong>license.framework</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.server</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.dsls.server.mode</strong></p></li></ul></li><li><p>For the FlexNet server:</p><ul><li><p><strong>license.framework</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>license.server<br /></strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7db8618e-5799-4302-9e90-687cd73d1271"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Cameo Collaborator supports the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=flexnet-three-server-redundancy---triad-license-server&amp;version=latest">FlexNet Three-Server Redundancy mode (Triad License Server)</a>. If you want to use the Triad License Server, specify the <strong>license.server</strong> property in this format:</p><p>license.server=&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server1_address&gt;,&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server2_address&gt;,&lt;port&gt;@&lt;server3_address&gt;</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="bde1e40d-01cc-4b5f-8b14-4ca763537e84"><p>Restart Web Application Platform.</p></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247063427 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00320: (2026x) Case study

- page_id: `247063427`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247063427/2026x+Case+study
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-14T11:25:17.637+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) User Guide > (2026x) Admin web applications > (2026x) Roles application > (2026x) Role-Based Access Control concept
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.

The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).

There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.

Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A specific role is assigned to each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.

Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Besides working on their respective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.

More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is **Global**. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also **Global**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The role scope of the user u7 and user u8 is Global.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.</p><p>The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy1.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).</h6><p>There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.</h6><p>Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A specific role is assigned to each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.</h6><p>Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy4.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Besides working on their respective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.</h6><p>More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is <strong>Global</strong>. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also <strong>Global</strong>.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy5.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The role scope of the user u7 and user u8 is Global.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247063649 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00321: (2026x) Cassandra and the product cluster setup

- page_id: `247063649`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247063649/2026x+Cassandra+and+the+product+cluster+setup
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-14T11:25:36.045+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) Installation > (2026x) Advanced installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page provides instructions to set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster on your system. A Teamwork Cloud cluster is composed of two clustering layers – Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra.

To set up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to perform two separate tasks:

1. 
2. 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An illustration of a Teamwork Cloud cluster and Cassandra cluster nodes within the Teamwork Cloud cluster.

##### Setting up a Cassandra cluster

Prior to establishing a Cassandra cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each node.
- Determine which node will be the contact point.

The minimum recommended cluster size for Cassandra is 3 nodes, when taking availability and redundancy into account. Cassandra's default read and write consistency is quorum. Replication factor should match the minimum node count of 3. It is recommended to scale up a cluster in multiples of 3.

When setting up a Cassandra node, you need to configure a seed that indicates an initial contact point for the new node. So, you can configure the contact point using the IP of any existing active node in the cluster. Use the node's own IP when configuring the first node. For example, if you are configuring a three-node cluster and the IPs of the nodes are*10.1.1.101*,*10.1.1.102,*and*10.1.1.103,*select one of them (e.g.*10.1.1.101*) as the**seed**. When configuring**cassandra.yaml**, specify*10.1.1.101*as the contact point value for all of the three nodes.

| Machine IP | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| seeds | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 |
| listen_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| broadcast_rpc_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |

Note that

listen_address

and

broadcast_rpc_address

are still set to their respective machine’s address

Now that you understand what a seed is and how to configure it, follow the instructions to install and configure Cassandra on Windows or Linux operating system.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Installing Cassandra on Windows' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Installing Apache Cassandra' space='']

After installing and configuring all nodes, start the seed node. Verify that it is operational by issuing the command "nodetool status".

Once the seed node is operational, you can proceed to start the other nodes in the cluster. You must allow the node to join the cluster before proceeding to the next node. After all nodes have started, the "nodetool status" command will display results as shown below.

```text

```

#### NOTE: Node Consistency

Node Consistency

In order to ensure that all nodes are consistent, please run the "nodetool repair" command nightly. This can be scheduled as a crontab job or via Windows Task Scheduler. This only needs to be executed on a single node.

###### **Cassandra configuration parameters**

*-***esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic.contact-points**

The value of this parameter is a list of Cassandra node IP addresses. You can find this parameter in **application.conf**by looking for the following.

```text

```

*-***esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace.replication-factor**

This parameter defines the Cassandra replication factor which describes how many copies of your data will be written by Cassandra. For example, replication factor 2 means your data will be written to 2 nodes.

For a three-node cluster, if you would like the cluster to be able to survive 1 node loss, you will need to set the replication factor to 3.

```text

```

Please note that this configuration will be used only for the first time falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcconnects to Cassandra and creates a new twc keyspace. Changing the replication factor after the keyspace has already been created is rather a complex task. Read [this document](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cql/3.3/cql/cql_using/useUpdateKeyspaceRF.html) if you need to change it.

falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcuses QUORUM for both write and read consistency levels.

[Click here](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra/3.0/cassandra/dml/dmlAboutDataConsistency.html) for a detailed explanation of data consistency.

To start up the Teamwork Cloud cluster, start the server on the seed machine and wait until you see a message similar to the following in the **server.log**.

```text

```

Then you can start the server on the remaining machines. You should see the following messages in the **server.log** which shows all 3 nodes are forming the cluster.

```text

```

##### Setting up a product cluster

Before setting up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each Teamwork Cloud node.
- Prepare a list of IPs of Cassandra nodes.

The instructions below use the following sample environment for ease of understanding.

- 3 nodes Teamwork Cloud cluster.
- Teamwork Cloud node IP addresses are 10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112 , and 10.1.1.113.
- All nodes will be used as seed nodes.
- IP addresses of Cassandra nodes are 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102, 10.1.1.103.

Setting up the Teamwork Cloud cluster involves 2 groups of parameters in **application.conf**.

###### Teamwork Cloud clustering parameter

*-***akka.cluster.seed-nodes**

This parameter indicates the initial seed for the cluster.

If you install falseMagic Collaboration StudiomcsfalseTeamwork Cloudtwcusing the installer file, you will be asked to provide the seed node IP during installation process and the value will be configured in **application.conf**.

If you install using the zip file, you will need to manually configure the parameter in****application.conf**. Search for the following:

```text

```

Replace *${seed-node.ip}*with the IP addresses of the seed nodes, so it should look similar to the following:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

For example, replication factor 2 means your data will be written to 2 nodes.</p><p>For a three-node cluster, if you would like the cluster to be able to survive 1 node loss, you will need to set the replication factor to 3. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ca7bf66d-9414-408c-ad38-4b3c0ef35458"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[persistence {

Changing the replication factor after the keyspace has already been created is rather a complex task. Read <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cql/3.3/cql/cql_using/useUpdateKeyspaceRF.html">this document</a> if you need to change it.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e13764cb-5f63-4445-b3ab-7bba06e12951"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a98d8a71-09dc-40f6-a510-82256ffbb106"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Magic Collaboration Studio</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">mcs</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d457906-e12a-4d96-8933-634b08de16ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Teamwork Cloud</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">twc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></span>uses QUORUM for both write and read consistency levels.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra/3.0/cassandra/dml/dmlAboutDataConsistency.html">Click here</a> for a detailed explanation of data consistency.</p><p>To start up the Teamwork Cloud cluster, start the server on the seed machine and wait until you see a message similar to the following in the <strong>server.log</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f276482-11df-41b8-adc2-991a35f44f62"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[INFO  2017-02-15 10:57:08.409 Teamwork Cloud cluster with 1 node(s) : [10.1.1.111] [com.nomagic.esi.server.core.actor.ClusterHealthActor, twcloud-esi.actor.other-dispatcher-31]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Then you can start the server on the remaining machines. twcloud-esi.actor.other-dispatcher-18]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Setting up a product cluster</h3><p>Before setting up a Teamwork Cloud cluster, you need to determine the following.</p><ul><li>The initial number of nodes in the cluster.</li><li>The IP address of each Teamwork Cloud node.</li><li>Prepare a list of IPs of Cassandra nodes.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>The instructions below use the following sample environment for ease of understanding.</p><ul><li>3 nodes Teamwork Cloud cluster.</li><li>Teamwork Cloud node IP addresses are <em>10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112</em>, and <em>10.1.1.113.</em></li><li>All nodes will be used as seed nodes.</li><li>IP addresses of Cassandra nodes are <em>10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102, 10.1.1.103.</em></li></ul><p><br /></p><p>Setting up the Teamwork Cloud cluster involves 2 groups of parameters in <strong>application.conf</strong>.</p><h4>Teamwork Cloud clustering parameter</h4><p><em>- </em><strong>akka.cluster.seed-nodes</strong></p><p>This parameter indicates the initial seed for the cluster.</p><p>If you install <span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="853d3981-95f1-47d5-87fe-a03751a9c6aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Magic Collaboration Studio</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">mcs</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="scroll-conditional-content-inline" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7d60ad23-52a9-4205-bf11-e2ff2914dd89"><ac:parameter ac:name="renderForPublishing">false</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="content">Teamwork Cloud</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="labels">twc</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></span>using the installer file, you will be asked to provide the seed node IP during installation process and the value will be configured in <strong>application.conf</strong>.</p><p>If you install using the zip file, you will need to manually configure the parameter in<em> </em><strong>application.conf</strong>. Search for the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8352ec9-5ab7-4507-9aac-c749a7d3aa57"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed-nodes = ["akka://twcloud@${seed-node.ip}:2552"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Replace <em>${seed-node.ip}</em><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">with the IP addresses of the seed nodes, so it should look similar to the following:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b0f896d-e1ba-4460-b63c-990b3a372a79"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed-nodes = ["akka://twcloud@10.1.1.111:2552","akka://twcloud@10.1.1.112:2552","akka://twcloud@10.1.1.113:2552"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=247063783 space=MCS version=1 -->
## PAGE 00322: (2026x) Cassandra cluster operations

- page_id: `247063783`
- space_key: `MCS`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS/pages/247063783/2026x+Cassandra+cluster+operations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-14T11:25:41.705+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Magic Collaboration Studio / Teamwork Cloud and Services > (2026x) Deployment Guide > (2026x) Installation > (2026x) Advanced installation
- labels: ['mcs', 'twc']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Replacing the down node

To replace a down/unresponsive node with a new node (different address) in the cluster, prepare the replacement node configuration to match other cluster nodes. Then, start the replacement node by specifying the dead node to replace with.

Prepare the replacement node by configuring it with the same cluster name, seed nodes, and data center name as other nodes in the cluster.

Add the following parameter to JVM_OPTS in *cassandra-env.sh* (default location: */etc/cassandra/conf*) for the replacement node:

```text
]]>
```

This parameter will drop the dead node and add the new node to the cluster upon startup. Remove the parameter after successful startup and replacement node joins the cluster.

To monitor the state and progress of replacement operations (including move and remove), use this command to display the progress of streaming operations:

```bash

```

##### Dropping node

Before dropping a node from a live cluster, make sure there are enough live nodes in the cluster to maintain replication factor. If dropping a node that is down, refer to [Replace Down Node] procedure.

To drop a live node, run the following command while on the node:

```bash

```

To drop a dead/offline node, run the following command:

```bash
]]>
```

If the dropped node was a seed node, update cassandra.yaml and reload seeds on all live nodes by executing the following command:

```bash

```

Otherwise, Teamwork Cloud service may not start.

##### Modifying the seed node

If a seed node was added, removed, or replaced from a cluster, the seed node list will need to be updated.

Use the following command to check what seeds are currently configured for the cluster:

```bash

```

Update the *seeds* list configuration, under *seed_provider* of *cassandra.yaml*, for every node in the cluster. Use the following command to apply the seed list change on a live node.

```bash

```

Otherwise, the changes will not be applied until each node is restarted.

##### Syncing nodes

The Cassandra repair tool is used to fix data inconsistencies between nodes. If a down node was replaced, repair will stream differences in the data from out-of-sync data between nodes.

For incremental repair of the current node, use the command:

```bash

```

Incremental repairs should be run regularly on each node. Apache recommends doing this every 7 days.

For a full repair of all data, use the command:

```bash

```

A new node to the cluster should bootstrap and sync data from other nodes. If a new node has not streamed data from other nodes on the cluster, use the following command to initiate the process:

```bash

```

For additional information, refer to the [official Cassandra documentation](https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/4.1/index.html).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Replacing the down node</h3><p><span>To replace a down/unresponsive node with a new node (different address) in the cluster, prepare the replacement node configuration to match other cluster nodes. Then, start the replacement node by specifying the dead node to replace with.</span></p><p><span>Prepare the replacement node by configuring it with the same cluster name, seed nodes, and data center name as other nodes in the cluster.</span></p><p><span>Add the following parameter to JVM_OPTS in <em>cassandra-env.sh</em> (default location: <em>/etc/cassandra/conf</em>) for the replacement node:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9db496b5-a4a1-48f7-87a1-3891eb11abcf"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcassandra.replace_address_first_boot=<dead_node_ip>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>This parameter will drop the dead node and add the new node to the cluster upon startup. Remove the parameter after successful startup and replacement node joins the cluster.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To monitor the state and progress of replacement operations (including move and remove), use this command to display the progress of streaming operations:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="deaf9a63-18e4-473c-b01f-d23c438c0b33"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool netstats]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Dropping node</h3><p><span>Before dropping a node from a live cluster, make sure there are enough live nodes in the cluster to maintain replication factor. If dropping a node that is down, refer to [Replace Down Node] procedure.</span></p><p><span>To drop a live node, run the following command while on the node:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f51aec9-ea78-4d51-899a-e1b078f2a2f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool decommission]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>To drop a dead/offline node, run the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24993ddf-c7ac-49b9-8878-59ee2165c1f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[nodetool removenode <Host ID>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span>If the dropped node was a seed node, update cassandra.yaml and reload seeds on all live nodes by executing the following
````
